behaviors.description: Conversation history trimmed to most recent 10 messages when full history exceeds context window
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives entirely in the trimming algorithm — selecting the last N messages from a list. No module boundary crossing is involved. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess needed. LLM-DEP: The trimming logic is deterministic; the LLM never needs to run. A unit test passing a list of 15 messages and asserting the output contains only the final 10 will catch any real failure. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: escalation would only be warranted if the trim boundary depended on a live tokenizer or a shared context-window service — the behavior description says nothing of the sort.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a mock conversation history of 15 messages, call the history-trimming function, assert the result contains exactly the 10 most recent messages in order, and assert the 5 oldest messages are absent.

behaviors.description: Generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'Addresses the customer's question' is a quality judgment about real LLM output. A mocked LLM trivially returns whatever stub we supply, so the test always passes regardless of actual model behavior — MOCK-HIDE is fully active here. Running this 100 times with a real model could yield on-topic responses most of the time but off-topic ones occasionally, which is exactly the failure mode this behavior describes. REAL-INFRA and MULTI-STEP do not escalate beyond Agentic: this is a single generation call, no subprocess or OS dependency. REJECTION-GATE for Workflow: there is no multi-step agentic sequence; the tone re-check is a separate behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Send a concrete customer question (e.g., 'How do I reset my password?') to the real chatbot endpoint backed by the real LLM, and assert the response contains content semantically related to password reset — not a generic greeting or unrelated topic. Run with a deterministic seed or low-temperature setting if the API supports it to reduce noise.

behaviors.description: Tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'Correctly identifies' is an accuracy claim about a classification step. If the tone re-check is an LLM call (as implied by 're-prompts the LLM'), mocking it always returns the desired label and hides real misclassification — MOCK-HIDE is active. The correctness of the label depends on real model behavior, making Agentic the floor. MOCK-MISS for Integration alone: the orchestration that 'triggers regeneration' could be tested at Integration by injecting a stubbed tone classifier, but that would not catch the 'correctly identifies' part. Both failure modes are claimed in this single behavior, so the higher requirement (Agentic) governs. REJECTION-GATE for Workflow: triggering one regeneration is a single conditional branch, not a multi-step agentic sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Submit an intentionally overly formal LLM response to the real tone-checking step with a casual-tier customer context, and assert (a) the classifier labels it as too-formal, and (b) a regeneration call is issued. Use a real LLM for the tone check; stub the initial generation to supply the known-formal draft.

behaviors.description: Full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives at the boundary between the chatbot orchestrator and the persistence layer — schema mismatches, missing fields, wrong timestamp format. A pure unit test with a mocked DB always passes even when the actual insert call is wrong. REAL-INFRA: A local/in-process test database (e.g., SQLite or an in-memory Mongo) is sufficient to catch schema and timestamp bugs; a production external service is not required. LLM-DEP: We can inject deterministic stub responses for both the initial generation and any regenerations — the LLM never needs to be real to test persistence correctness. REJECTION-GATE for System: a local DB captures the relevant failure; no OS-level or real-network dependency is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the chatbot orchestrator to a local test database with a seeded timestamp source. Inject a stub initial response and a stub regeneration, run the full exchange, then query the database and assert: the user message, both responses (draft and final), and monotonically increasing timestamps are all present with correct field names and types.

behaviors.description: When tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — customer never sees the rejected draft
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is at the orchestration layer: the logic that decides which response object is forwarded to the caller versus which is suppressed. This is a multi-module interaction (tone checker result → response router → caller return value), so Unit is too low — a single function boundary will not expose misrouting. REAL-INFRA: No OS, subprocess, or real network needed; stubs for both LLM calls (returning a controllable 'formal' first response and an acceptable second) suffice. LLM-DEP: Correctness here is about routing logic, not LLM output quality — we inject both responses deterministically and assert the second is what reaches the caller. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: mocking both LLM calls to return predefined drafts does NOT hide the failure; the failure is in the replacement logic, not in model behavior.
behaviors.test_description: With the LLM mocked to return a predefined formal draft on the first call and an acceptable draft on the second, trigger a full chatbot exchange for a casual-tier customer. Assert that the value returned to the caller is the second response, not the first, and that any API surface (return value, response DTO) contains no trace of the rejected draft.
